title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Lily Carter, who runs street art walking tours.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today I'm talking to Lily Carter, who runs walking tours about street art. Lily, how did you become interested in street art? || Guest: I studied photography, and I used to take pictures of old buildings. One day I photographed a wall painting before it was painted over the next week. [20]That made me realise street art could disappear quickly, so I wanted to record and understand it[/20]. Soon I was reading about the artists and following changes in different streets. || Interviewer: What makes a good walking tour? || Guest: People need information, but they also need time to look. I don't want to talk all the time. [21]A good tour includes short stories, quiet moments and a route that doesn't make people feel rushed[/21]. Comfortable shoes help too, but the pace matters most. || Interviewer: Do visitors usually know a lot about street art? || Guest: Some do, but many join because they are curious. [22]I enjoy tours most when people arrive saying they don't understand street art and leave with favourite pieces[/22]. You don't need expert knowledge to have an opinion. You just need to look carefully. || Interviewer: Do local people like the tours? || Guest: Most are positive, especially shop owners who enjoy the extra visitors. But I always remind groups to be respectful. [23]The biggest complaint from residents is not the art itself, but tourists blocking pavements while taking photos[/23]. So I keep the group small and move them aside when we stop. || Interviewer: Has the street art in your city changed? || Guest: Yes. Years ago, much of it was unofficial and appeared overnight. Now some walls are planned with permission. [24]I think the best change is that more local artists are being invited to create public work[/24], rather than only famous visitors from other cities. || Interviewer: What are you developing next? || Guest: I am working on an online map, but it is taking longer than expected because the art changes so fast. [25]Before that, I'm launching a shorter tour for families with children, with more time for drawing activities[/25]. I hope it helps young people notice the streets around them. || Interviewer: Do you think street art should always be protected? || Guest: Not always. Part of its character is that it changes. A wall may have one picture this month and something completely different next month. That can be sad, but it also keeps the streets alive. I think the most important works should be recorded properly, even if they cannot stay forever. || Interviewer: How do you choose the route for a tour? || Guest: I walk it myself at different times of day. I check traffic, pavements and places where the group can stop safely. I also make sure there is a café or public toilet nearby, because practical details can make the difference between a pleasant tour and a tiring one. || Interviewer: What do you hope visitors remember after a tour? || Guest: I hope they look up more often. Many of us walk through streets without noticing walls, windows or small details. If visitors leave the tour and begin noticing their own neighbourhood differently, then the tour has done something useful.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today I'm talking to Lily Carter, who runs walking tours about street art. Lily, how did you become interested in street art?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I studied photography, and I used to take pictures of old buildings. One day I photographed a wall painting before it was painted over the next week. That made me realise street art could disappear quickly, so I wanted to record and understand it. Soon I was reading about the artists and following changes in different streets.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What makes a good walking tour?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: People need information, but they also need time to look. I don't want to talk all the time. A good tour includes short stories, quiet moments and a route that doesn't make people feel rushed. Comfortable shoes help too, but the pace matters most.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do visitors usually know a lot about street art?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Some do, but many join because they are curious. I enjoy tours most when people arrive saying they don't understand street art and leave with favourite pieces. You don't need expert knowledge to have an opinion. You just need to look carefully.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do local people like the tours?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Most are positive, especially shop owners who enjoy the extra visitors. But I always remind groups to be respectful. The biggest complaint from residents is not the art itself, but tourists blocking pavements while taking photos. So I keep the group small and move them aside when we stop.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Has the street art in your city changed?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes. Years ago, much of it was unofficial and appeared overnight. Now some walls are planned with permission. I think the best change is that more local artists are being invited to create public work, rather than only famous visitors from other cities.

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I am working on an online map, but it is taking longer than expected because the art changes so fast. Before that, I'm launching a shorter tour for families with children, with more time for drawing activities. I hope it helps young people notice the streets around them.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you think street art should always be protected?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Not always. Part of its character is that it changes. A wall may have one picture this month and something completely different next month. That can be sad, but it also keeps the streets alive. I think the most important works should be recorded properly, even if they cannot stay forever.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you choose the route for a tour?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I walk it myself at different times of day. I check traffic, pavements and places where the group can stop safely. I also make sure there is a café or public toilet nearby, because practical details can make the difference between a pleasant tour and a tiring one.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you hope visitors remember after a tour?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I hope they look up more often. Many of us walk through streets without noticing walls, windows or small details. If visitors leave the tour and begin noticing their own neighbourhood differently, then the tour has done something useful.

questions.question: Why did Lily first become interested in street art?

options.A: She saw that it could disappear very quickly.
options.B: She wanted to become a street artist herself.
options.C: She studied it as part of a photography course.

questions.explanation: Lily says seeing a wall painting disappear made her realise street art could disappear quickly.

questions.question: What does Lily say makes a good walking tour?

options.A: A fast route through many streets.
options.B: A balance of stories and time to look.
options.C: A guide who talks almost all the time.

questions.explanation: Lily says a good tour has short stories, quiet moments and a route that does not feel rushed.

questions.question: What does Lily enjoy most about guiding tours?

options.A: Meeting people who already know the artists.
options.B: Helping people discover works they like.

questions.explanation: Lily says she enjoys when people arrive not understanding street art and leave with favourite pieces.

questions.question: What problem do some residents mention?

options.A: Visitors standing in the way to take photos.
options.C: Tour groups making noise late at night.

questions.explanation: Lily says residents complain about tourists blocking pavements while taking photos.

questions.question: What change in street art does Lily like best?

options.B: More famous artists visit the city.
options.C: More local artists are asked to make public art.

questions.explanation: Lily says the best change is that more local artists are being invited to create public work.

questions.question: What will Lily offer soon?

options.A: A family tour with drawing activities.
options.B: An online map of every painting.

questions.explanation: Lily says she is launching a shorter tour for families with children, with drawing activities.
